variables.content: The DMC-calculated quasiparticle effective mass exhibits opposite trends with density for the two spin polarizations studied: in the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG $m^{*}$ increases with increasing $r_{s}$ (decreasing density), producing a significant enhancement at $r_{s}=5$ and $r_{s}=10$, whereas in the fully ferromagnetic (fully spin-polarized) 2D HEG $m^{*}$ decreases as density is lowered; these calculated trends are quantitatively consistent with the experimental observations of Tan et al. for paramagnetic systems and of Padmanabhan et al. for ferromagnetic or partially spin-polarized systems.

Extrapolating the finite-size DMC effective-mass data to the thermodynamic limit using the single-power-law scaling with exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) yields the following quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) expressed in Hartree atomic units (a.u.): for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\); for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.851(5)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=0.74(1)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=0.70(3)\\). These numerical values are the principal thermodynamic-limit benchmarks reported for clean, continuum-model 2D HEGs in this work.

For the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG over the density-parameter range \\(r_{s}=1\\) to 10, let \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) denote the quasiparticle effective mass extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit by fitting the finite-size DMC effective masses to the single-power-law form \\(m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma}\\) and adopting the exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) for the extrapolation. The proposition is: the extrapolated numerical values \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\) indicate that the true thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass of the clean continuum-model paramagnetic 2D HEG remains close to unity across this \\(r_{s}\\) range and that there is no significant enhancement of \\(m^{*}\\) at low density for this physical system class.

For a sequence of computed finite-size quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(N)\\) obtained by DMC for a 2D HEG at fixed density and spin polarization over the available range of system sizes \\(N\\), the proposition is: the dependence of \\(m^{*}(N)\\) on \\(N\\) can be modelled to good approximation by a single-term power-law finite-size correction,
      $$
      m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma},
      $$
      with approximately constant parameters \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\), \\(b\\), and exponent \\(\\gamma\\) across the sampled \\(N\\)-range, so that this parametric form provides a valid basis for extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit within the statistical precision of the DMC data.

Let the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG be represented theoretically by the continuum-model DMC calculations and experimentally by high-quality semiconductor two-dimensional systems in which spin polarization can be induced (for example by applying an in-plane magnetic field). The proposition is: the suppression of the quasiparticle effective mass with decreasing density observed in the DMC-extrapolated ferromagnetic results (numerically \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.851(5)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=0.74(1)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=0.70(3)\\)) corresponds qualitatively to the trend measured in experiments cited in the literature (for example Padmanabhan et al.), and this qualitative agreement is not invalidated by differences in sample specifics, partial polarization, disorder, or experimental measurement methodology.

The occupied bandwidth $\Delta\mathcal{E}=\mathcal{E}(k_F)-\mathcal{E}(0)$ of the 2D HEG was obtained from the quartic fits to the DMC bands at $r_s=1$, $5$, and $10$ atomic units; the DMC bandwidths (in Hartree atomic units) are: paramagnetic $\Delta\mathcal{E}=1.045(5)$ at $r_s=1$, $0.0281(8)$ at $r_s=5$, and $0.0055(3)$ at $r_s=10$; ferromagnetic $\Delta\mathcal{E}=2.434(6)$ at $r_s=1$, $0.141(1)$ at $r_s=5$, and $0.0427(8)$ at $r_s=10$. Here $k_F$ denotes the Fermi wave vector, with $k_F=\sqrt{2}/r_s$ for the paramagnetic 2D fluid and $k_F=2/r_s$ for the fully spin-polarized 2D fluid, and the free-electron occupied bandwidth is $\Delta\mathcal{E}^{\mathrm{free}}=k_F^2/2$. These DMC bandwidths lie substantially below the Hartree-Fock (HF) bandwidth values computed as $\Delta\mathcal{E}^{\mathrm{HF}}=k_F^2/2 + k_F(1-2/\pi)$, and for the paramagnetic fluid at the intermediate and low densities studied ($r_s=5$ and $10$) the DMC bandwidths are smaller than the free-electron bandwidth $\Delta\mathcal{E}^{\mathrm{free}}$, whereas for the ferromagnetic fluid the DMC bandwidths exceed the free-electron bandwidth at the densities studied.

For paramagnetic 3D-HEG calculations performed in a finite periodic simulation cell containing N = 178 electrons, the Slater-Jastrow (SJ) trial wave function—defined as a single Slater determinant of plane-wave orbitals multiplied by a Jastrow correlation factor—and the Slater-Jastrow-backflow (SJB) trial wave function—the SJ form with additional backflow-transformed quasiparticle coordinates—yield very similar diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) quasiparticle effective masses m*. Specifically, at density parameter r_s = 1 (r_s = ((3/(4π n))^{1/3} a_B^{-1})), the DMC values are m* = 0.915(1) with the SJ trial WF and m* = 0.921(1) with the SJB trial WF, and at r_s = 10 the DMC values are m* = 0.75(1) (SJ) and m* = 0.78(1) (SJB); these numerical results indicate that, for the paramagnetic 3D-HEG at N = 178 and the densities studied, inclusion of backflow in the trial WF produces only small quantitative changes in the DMC effective mass.

The quasiparticle effective masses m^{*} reported in the literature from DMC fits to the single-particle energy band and the ground-state total-energy parametrizations reported in the literature as continuous functions E_{\mathrm{corr}}(r_s,\zeta) of density parameter r_s and spin polarization \zeta are mutually consistent within their stated uncertainties and can be combined algebraically via standard Fermi-liquid thermodynamic relations (for example F_1^{s} = m^{*}-1 for the paramagnetic HEG and evaluation of F_0^{s,a} from derivatives of E(r_s,\zeta)) to produce numerical estimates of the dimensionless Fermi liquid parameters F_l for the 2D HEG.
      Refs. 11,23,24

Let $E(R_{cut})$ be the DMC total energy per electron for the homogeneous electron gas (HEG) at density parameter $r_s$ and let $E_\infty$ be the converged energy per electron. For the HEG calculations performed with nonorthogonal orbitals constructed and DMC settings as described above, the truncation $R_{cut}=7.0$ (units consistent with the HEG data reported) at $r_s=2$ produces an energy difference $E(R_{cut})-E_\infty$ of approximately $2\ \mathrm{meV}$ per electron, with this numeric reading subject to the typical plotting, finite-sample, and basis-supercell uncertainties inherent in the reported data.

Consider the reported DMC calculations carried out with finite DMC time steps (time steps of 0.04, 0.2, and 0.4 a.u. at \\(r_{s}=1,5,10\\) for paramagnetic HEGs and 0.01, 0.2, and 0.4 a.u. at \\(r_{s}=1,5,10\\) for ferromagnetic HEGs) and using single-determinant Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave functions that, according to extrapolation of variational Monte Carlo energies to zero energy variance, retrieve more than 99% of the correlation energy. The proposition is: the residual biases arising from the fixed-node approximation and from finite DMC time-step errors (noting that halving the time step was verified to have a negligible effect on the computed energy band) are small compared with the overall uncertainties introduced by the finite-size extrapolation procedure; therefore these residual numerical biases do not materially shift the reported extrapolated thermodynamic-limit effective masses \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\).
